talk_title: Clonal hematopoiesis, TET2 deficiency and IL-1β inhibition in lung cancer

speaker.name: Janghee Wu
speaker.affiliation: Emory University Winship Cancer Institute, Department of Hematology

targets.dependency_context: TET2 mutated clonal hematopoiesis in NSCLC

biomarkers.name: TET2 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with decreased lung cancer incidence in canakinumab-treated patients

biomarkers.name: Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) status
biomarkers.finding: 24% of neoadjuvant trial patients carried CH mutations

models.name: KP orthotopic lung cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: TET2 heterozygous or homozygous knockout, young (10-12 wk) vs aged (18-20 mo) mice

models.name: REAL-NADIM neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Early-stage NSCLC patients, CH+ vs CH-

models.name: 59 NSCLC patient mononuclear cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: CH mutation profiling, scRNA-seq of >200k cells

clinical_data.value: significantly prolonged in CH+ patients after neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy
no significant difference between CH+ and CH-
no significant difference between CH+ and CH-

MOA_summary: TET2-deficient clonal hematopoiesis skews the immune microenvironment toward immunosuppression, increasing myeloid and exhausted T cell populations, which promotes lung tumor development; IL-1β inhibition with canakinumab reduces cancer incidence in CH-negative mice but not in TET2-mutant contexts, highlighting a gene‑environment interaction.
key_findings: TET2-deficient hematopoiesis accelerates lung tumor growth in aged mice but not in young mice.
Aged TET2‑mutant mice show increased tumor‑infiltrating CD45+ cells, reduced NK cells, and exhausted PD‑1+ CD8 T cells.
Human NSCLC patients with TET2‑mutated CH display similar immune remodeling (increased monocytes, reduced NK cells).
In the REAL‑NADIM neoadjuvant trial, CH+ patients had significantly longer overall survival after chemoimmunotherapy despite similar pathologic response and PFS.
CH clones expand over disease course, especially in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TET2 mutation confers resistance to IL‑1β blockade in later‑stage lung cancer (CANOPE study).

combination_strategies.rationale: Test whether IL‑1β inhibition mitigates CH‑driven tumor promotion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced lung cancer incidence in CH‑negative mice; no benefit in TET2‑mutant mice

open_questions: Does clonal hematopoiesis acquire new mutations after therapy, converting blood‑only CH to tumor‑infiltrating CH?
What are the mechanisms by which TET2‑mutant CH cells remodel the tumor microenvironment beyond cytokine secretion?

talk_title: Tumor‑infiltrating clonal hematopoiesis (TICH) in the TRACERx NSCLC cohort

speaker.name: Aino-Maija Leppa
speaker.affiliation: University of Cambridge, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: TICH status
biomarkers.finding: Patients with tumor‑infiltrating CH have worse disease‑free survival than CH‑negative patients

models.name: TRACERx NSCLC cohort (completed)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 800 patients, 33% CH prevalence, 43% of CH infiltrates tumors

models.name: 37 patients with longitudinal plasma cfDNA
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tracked CHIP clone VAF over time

MOA_summary: Clonal hematopoiesis clones, especially those with DNMT3A or TET2 mutations, can infiltrate NSCLC tumors (TICH), where they expand preferentially and remodel the immune microenvironment toward an inflammatory, interferon‑rich state, leading to poorer recurrence‑free survival, particularly in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
key_findings: 33% of TRACERx NSCLC patients harbor CH mutations; 43% of those infiltrate tumors (TICH).
TICH patients have significantly reduced disease‑free survival compared with CH‑negative patients.
CH clones expand over time in patients who do not receive adjuvant therapy.
TET2 and DNMT3A mutant clones achieve larger relative tumor‑infiltrating clone sizes than other CH mutations.
Single‑cell analyses reveal TET2‑mutant cells enriched in myeloid lineages and NK cells, while TICH tumors show increased γδ T cells, cycling T cells, and interferon response genes.
open_questions: Do CH clones acquire additional driver mutations during disease progression?
What therapeutic strategies can specifically target TICH‑associated immune remodeling?

talk_title: TFF2 deficiency amplifies IL‑1β inflammation and drives aging‑associated gastric tumor progression

targets.dependency_context: MDSC‑derived cytokine in aged gastric tumors

biomarkers.name: PMN‑MDSC frequency
biomarkers.finding: Increased in aged stomach and blood; reduced after TFF2‑MSA treatment

biomarkers.name: CD8+ T cell proportion
biomarkers.finding: Decreased in aged mice; partially restored by TFF2‑MSA

models.name: TFF2 heterozygous/knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: TFF2+/‑ and TFF2‑/‑, young (3 mo) vs aged (18 mo)

models.name: ACKP orthotopic gastric tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Implanted into young vs aged mice

models.name: MNU‑induced gastric cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Carcinogen‑induced tumors in young vs aged mice

MOA_summary: Aging reduces gastric TFF2‑expressing epithelial cells, leading to expansion of PMN‑MDSCs that secrete IL‑1β, which activates IL‑1R1‑positive CAFs and creates an immunosuppressive, tumor‑promoting niche; systemic administration of a stabilized TFF2‑MSA fusion reverses MDSC expansion, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and suppresses tumor growth in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mouse stomachs show a marked loss of TFF2⁺ epithelial cells.
Aging drives systemic inflammaging with increased PMN‑MDSCs, bone‑marrow myeloid bias, and elevated IL‑6/TNF‑α.
TFF2‑deficient mice recapitulate the aged immune phenotype (MDSC expansion, CD8⁺ T cell reduction).
TFF2‑MSA treatment reduces MDSC accumulation, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and normalizes bone‑marrow myelopoiesis.
In both ACKP orthotopic and MNU‑induced gastric cancer models, aged mice develop larger tumors and shorter survival, which are mitigated by TFF2‑MSA pretreatment.

combination_strategies.rationale: TFF2‑MSA synergizes with checkpoint inhibition in prior studies
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor suppression in aged mice

open_questions: Does TFF2‑MSA affect other stromal compartments beyond CAFs?
Can TFF2‑MSA improve responses to standard chemotherapy in aged gastric cancer models?

talk_title: Aging‑mediated reactivation of metastatic melanoma via γδ T‑cell dysfunction

speaker.name: Kelly Coutant
speaker.affiliation: University of California, Postdoctoral Fellow in Mitchison Lab

biomarkers.name: γδ T‑cell frequency
biomarkers.finding: Decreased in middle‑aged mice; associated with increased liver metastasis

models.name: C57BL/6 middle‑age (12‑mo) and geriatric (24‑mo) mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tail‑vein or hydrodynamic tail‑vein injection of melanoma cells

models.name: γδ T‑cell depletion model (DTR‑expressing γδ T cells + diphtheria toxin)

MOA_summary: Aging creates a liver microenvironment that reduces functional γδ T cells, increases POSN1 expression in melanoma cells, and expands PMN‑MDSCs, collectively driving immunosuppressive γδ T‑cell phenotypes (PD‑1⁺, Vγ1⁺) and promoting metastatic outgrowth; depletion of γδ T cells further exacerbates metastasis, confirming their protective role in young hosts.
key_findings: Middle‑aged mice develop significantly more liver metastases than young or geriatric mice.
γδ T‑cell numbers drop specifically in middle‑aged livers, correlating with metastasis burden.
Depleting γδ T cells reduces primary tumor growth but increases liver metastasis, indicating site‑specific roles.
POSN1 overexpression in melanoma cells enhances both lung and liver metastasis and expands PMN‑MDSCs while decreasing γδ T‑cell infiltration.
Aged livers show increased arginase‑1 expression and MDSC accumulation, suggesting metabolic suppression of γδ T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aged γδ T cells display higher PD‑1 and Vγ1 (protumor) and lower Vγ6 (antitumor) expression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restore functional γδ T cells in aged hosts to limit metastasis

open_questions: What upstream signals drive POSN1 upregulation in aged melanoma cells?
Can senolytic targeting of MDSCs restore γδ T‑cell function in aged livers?

cited_papers: Mitchison et al. 2023 Nat Immunol (POSN1 study)

talk_title: Age‑stratified therapeutic strategies targeting chemo‑resistant subclones in triple‑negative breast cancer

speaker.name: M Spacek
speaker.affiliation: University of XYZ, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: Barcode 25 clone frequency
biomarkers.finding: Enriches after paclitaxel treatment in both young and aged mice

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Increased in tumors containing chemo‑resistant clone

models.name: FVB mice (young 8‑10 wk, aged 14 mo) with Met1 TNBC tumors
Met1 TNBC cells with 31 DNA‑barcoded subclones
models.genotype_or_key_features: Unique barcode per clone; barcode 25 chemo‑resistant

MOA_summary: A DNA‑barcode tracking system identifies a minor subpopulation (barcode 25) that survives paclitaxel, drives PD‑L1 up‑regulation, interferon‑γ signaling, EMT, and immunosuppression; pan‑HDAC inhibition selectively targets this clone, restoring chemosensitivity and enhancing anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy, especially in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mice respond less to paclitaxel and gain no additional benefit from anti‑PD‑L1 combination.
Only one barcode (25) expands after paclitaxel, conferring resistance in vitro and in vivo.
Barcode 25 tumors exhibit higher PD‑L1, CXCL10, vimentin, and EBI3 expression.
Pan‑HDAC inhibitor panobinostat is the top hit in a drug screen against barcode 25 cells.
Combining panobinostat with anti‑PD‑L1 markedly improves survival in aged mice, and triple therapy (paclitaxel + panobinostat + anti‑PD‑L1) achieves near‑complete control in young mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Chemo‑resistant clone constitutes <5% of tumor yet drives immunosuppressive phenotype.

combination_strategies.rationale: HDAC inhibition up‑regulates immune visibility of resistant clone
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor control in aged mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care chemotherapy; triple combo yields near‑complete response in young mice

open_questions: Will selective HDAC inhibitors retain efficacy with reduced toxicity in older patients?
What is the molecular mechanism by which HDAC inhibition overcomes barcode 25 resistance?

cited_papers: Spacek et al. 2024 Cancer Cell (DNA barcode platform)

talk_title: Age‑dependent neuroimmune changes in glioblastoma reveal senescent microglia as a therapeutic target

targets.dependency_context: Age‑related microglial senescence in GBM peritumoral brain

biomarkers.name: β‑galactosidase+ microglia
biomarkers.finding: Increased in extracranial brain of aged GBM mice; reduced by TQ senolytic

biomarkers.name: senescence score (gene signature)
biomarkers.finding: Higher in peritumoral region of older GBM patients, especially in microglia

models.name: C57BL/6 young (2 mo) vs old (24 mo) intracranial SB28 GBM model
Aged C57BL/6 immune‑depleted mice with GBM43 PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: Human GBM PDX in aged immunocompromised host

MOA_summary: Aging increases senescent microglia in the peritumoral brain, creating a pro‑inflammatory niche that limits the efficacy of radiation plus immunotherapy; senolytic treatment (dasatinib + quercetin) selectively clears these senescent microglia, enhancing survival in aged GBM models when combined with standard radiotherapy and immunotherapy.
key_findings: Patient glioblastoma cores show no age‑related transcriptional differences, but peritumoral tissue in older patients is enriched for senescent microglia.
Aged mice exhibit higher β‑galactosidase+ microglia in extracranial brain, not within the tumor core.
Senolytic TQ reduces senescent microglia in aged brains and improves survival when combined with radiation + anti‑PD‑1, but not in young mice.
In aged humanized GBM PDX mice, adding TQ to standard radiation + temozolomide further extends survival for a subset of animals.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Senolytic effect observed only in extracranial brain, not within tumor core, suggesting limited drug penetration or lower senescent cell burden inside tumor.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care induces tumor cell death; senolytic removes age‑associated immunosuppressive microglia
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved overall survival in aged mice

open_questions: Why does the senolytic fail to clear senescent cells within the tumor core?
Does radiation itself increase senescence in the peritumoral brain, thereby creating a therapeutic window for senolytics?
